text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon lifts the transverse colon superiorly, revealing a highly vascular mass suspected to be a GIST. Dissection begins at the superior aspect of the mass, exposing the second and third parts of the duodenum. The transverse colon is reflected superiorly, and the anterior surface of the duodenum becomes visible. Dissection progresses towards the inferior aspect of the mass, approaching the ileocolic junction. The ileocolic vessel is identified, and a supplying vessel of the tumor is tackled using a bipolar energy sealing device.

The surgeon carefully reflects the ileocolic vessels from the capsulated mass, observing the pulsation of the vessels. Dissection is performed at the inferior border of the mass, creating a window in the mesentery of the colon. The entry into the retroperitoneum is visible, with attention given to the numerous vessels surrounding the highly vascular tumor to prevent bleeding and protect surrounding structures.

The surgeon continues dissection on the anterior or superior aspect of the highly vascular tumor, revealing the transverse colon. Care is taken to preserve the vascular arcade of the colon while using a vessel sealing device and vascular forceps to maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon pushes the colon away, separating the attachment from the mass to the mesocolon. The lifted colon reveals the right colic vessel, which is divided using a ligature. Dissection continues on the superior aspect of the tumor.

The surgeon lifts and separates the colon, dividing the remaining attachments to the mesocolon with the left hand. The tumor is reflected medially, revealing the second and third parts of the duodenum at the D2-D3 junction. The base of the tumor is thinned out, minimizing the endotomy area. Monopolar cautery is used to divide the duodenum, with minor bleeding observed as the tumor is separated from the duodenum.

The surgical field is cleaned, and the surgeon closes a small entrotomy in the duodenum using 3-0 Vicryl sutures. The healthy normal mucosa is visible as the surgeon places a central stitch followed by additional stitches on either side. The duodenum is closed primarily with interrupted sutures, ensuring good blood supply for healing. The final sutures are being placed around the entrotomy site.

The completed suturing is visible in the clean surgical field. A drain is placed near the surgical area, and the specimen, identified as a high-grade GIST with more than five mitotic figures, is retrieved.
